turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 22-429, Acheson Hotels versus Laufer.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Deborah Laufer has sued hundreds of hotels whose websites failed to state whether they have rooms accessible to the disabled. As the sheer number of lawsuits suggests, Laufer does not focus her efforts on hotels where she has any thought of staying, much less booking a room. Instead, she systematically searches the web to find hotels that fail to provide accessibility information and sues to force compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act. Ordinarily hotels settle her claims and pay her attorney's fees. But some, like Acheson, the petitioner in this case, have resisted on the ground that Laufer is not injured by the absence of information about rooms she has no plans to reserve. Here's why that matters. Only plaintiffs who allege a concrete injury have standing to sue in Federal Court. Laufer, the argument goes, has not personally suffered an injury. Instead, she is a self-appointed tester plaintiff who sues to enforce the law rather than to remedy her own harms. Laufer's litigation has single-handedly generated a circuit split. Some circuits have held that she lacked standing to bring these suits. Others, including the First Circuit, which is the case that we've taken here, have held that she has it. After we granted review the case took an unusual turn. In July a District Court suspended Laufer's lawyer for defrauding hotels by lying in fee petitions and during settlement negotiations. Making matters worse, the report determined that Laufer's lawyer was funneling large sums to the father of Laufer's grandchild for investigatory work he never performed. That raised the prospect that both Laufer and her lawyer were getting a cut of the money. Following these unsavory revelations, Laufer voluntarily dismissed her pending suits with prejudice, including her complaint against Acheson in the District of Maine. She also filed a suggestion of mootness in this court. Laufer does not argue that we must dismiss her case for mootness. She acknowledges that we can address jurisdictional issues in any order that we choose. But mootness is easy and standing is hard, Laufer says, and she urges us to refrain from resolving a difficult question in a case that is otherwise over. Acheson on the other hand would like us to settle standing. Both the parties and this Court have already investigated significant resources in resolving that issue, which is not going away just because Laufer has dropped her case. The circuit split still exists and while Laufer may have hung up her litigating shoes, other plaintiffs will follow in her footsteps. Moreover, Acheson warns that dismissing this case from mootness will set a bad precedent. Why would any hotel take a case this far Acheson asks if the respondent can evade our review by abandoning a claim rather than risking a loss? We share Acheson's concern about allowing litigants to manipulate the jurisdiction of this Court, but we are not convinced that Laufer abandoned her case in an effort to evade our review. These are unusual circumstances. A lower court sanctioned Laufer's lawyer for misconduct that implicated Laufer herself and she has voluntarily dismissed her pending ADA cases and represented to this Court that she will not file any others. Her case against Acheson is moot and we dismiss it on that ground. We emphasize however that we might exercise our discretion differently in a future case. The judgment is vacated and the case is remanded to the United States Court of Appeals for the First Circuit with instructions to dismiss the case as moot. Justice Thomas and Justice Jackson have each filed an opinion concurring in the judgment.
